---
title: "Kick Off with Shiny for Python"
subtitle: "AthlyticZ Masterclass &mdash; 3 July 2026"
author: "Veerle Eeftink - van Leemput"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:
      - default
      - brand
      - custom.scss
    center: false
    transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    code-block-height: 400px
    slide-number: true
    footer: "Kick Off with Shiny for Python"
---

## Why are we still here? {.center}

It's 2026. Claude exists. GitHub Copilot exists.

. . .

**So why spend a day learning Shiny from scratch? 😅**

---

## LLMs are your pair programmer

You can't validate what Claude is producing if you have no foundational knowledge.

- Claude catches syntax, but **you** catch architecture
- Claude writes fast, but **you** know what's actually possible
- Claude tends to overshoot, but **you** know how to simplify
. . .

> The goal isn't to write every line yourself.
> The goal is to **know enough to steer**.

---

## Python has a lot of app frameworks {.center}

| Framework | Reactivity | Best for |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Streamlit** | Reruns top-to-bottom on every interaction | Quick data apps, ML demos |
| **Dash** | Explicit callback decorators linking inputs to outputs | Analytical dashboards, Plotly-heavy apps |
| **Gradio** | Event-driven, limited layout control | ML model demos |
| **Shiny** | Fine-grained: only what changed reruns | Production apps, complex state |

---

## Why Shiny?

- **Reactive by design**: inputs, outputs, and calculations form a dependency graph. Only what needs to update, updates.
- **Production-ready**: Posit-backed, Shiny for R has been in production for 10+ years. Python got it in 2022.
- **Full layout control**: Bootstrap-based UI, cards, sidebars, navbars, theming. Not just widgets stacked vertically.
- **One framework, two languages**: if you ever touched Shiny for R, you already know 80% of Shiny for Python.

---

## Two ways to write Shiny {.center}

Same reactive engine. Two different styles.

|  | **Core** | **Express** |
|--|----------|-------------|
| Structure | Explicit UI + server | Everything at the top level |
| Layout | Nested function calls | `with` context managers |
| Outputs | 2 steps: placeholder + render fn | 1 step: render fn places itself |
| Best for | Larger apps, complex layouts | Quick builds, demos |

We'll be using **Core** .

---

## Core: explicit structure

```python
from shiny import App, ui, render

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text("name", "Your name"),
    ui.output_text("greeting"),   # step 1: placeholder in UI
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    def greeting():               # step 2: render fn in server
        return f"Hello, {input.name()}!"

app = App(app_ui, server)
```
---

## Express: less boilerplate

```python
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

with ui.card():
    ui.input_text("name", "Your name")

    @render.text
    def greeting():               # defined once, placed here
        return f"Hello, {input.name()}!"
```

No `App()`. No separate server function. The render decorator places the output where you define it.

::: {.callout-note}
Source: [shiny.posit.co/py/docs/express-vs-core.html](https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/express-vs-core.html)
:::

---

## Python... Eh...

Yes yes, I'm an R person. Perhaps you are too.

So let's start with setting up our Python project.

## What is uv?

**uv** is a fast Python package and project manager — one tool that replaces `pip`, `venv`, and `pip-tools`.

- Written in Rust → 10–100× faster than pip
- Manages virtual environments for you automatically
- Locks exact dependency versions in `uv.lock`
- Made by Astral (the folks behind Ruff)

Install it once:

```bash
pip install uv
```

---

## Setting up a project with uv

**Initialize the project:**

```bash
uv init
```

The first time you run `uv run`, `uv sync`, or `uv lock`, uv also creates:

- `.venv/`: virtual environment in your project root
- `uv.lock`: lockfile pinning exact versions

---

## Managing your dependencies

**Add your dependencies:**

```bash
uv add requests shiny
```

**Lock them:**

```bash
uv lock
```

::: {.callout-note}
In **Positron**, the `!` prefix matters: Console pane (Python interpreter) → `!uv init`, `!uv add …`
Terminal pane → no `!` needed
:::

---

## Project setup

Three commands to go from zero to running app:

```bash
uv init my-app
cd my-app
uv add shiny requests
```

Run the app:

```bash
shiny run app.py --reload --launch-browser
```

That's it. `uv` handles the virtual env, the lockfile, everything.

---

## What we're building

A World Cup 2026 dashboard! ⚽

| Tab | What it shows |
|-----|---------------|
| **Live** | Yesterday / today / tomorrow matches, live scores |
| **Groups** | All 12 groups, with results (group phase has ended) |
| **Schedule** | Every past and upcoming match |

- `basic.py`: plain, functional, no styling
- `polished.py`: branded with `_brand.yml`, custom CSS, and a nice background

---

## The API

Base URL: `https://worldcup26.ir`

```
GET /get/teams   → all 48 teams, names + flag URLs
GET /get/groups  → standings for all 12 groups
GET /get/games   → every match, scores, phase, date
```

No auth required & fully open source.

```python
import requests

resp = requests.get("https://worldcup26.ir/get/groups")
data = resp.json()   # {"groups": [{"name": "A", "teams": [...]}]}
```

---

## reactive.calc: cache your data

`@reactive.calc` is like `@render`, but it **caches its result** and only re-runs when its inputs change.

```python
@reactive.calc
def all_groups():
    resp = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/get/groups")
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return {g["name"]: g["teams"] for g in resp.json()["groups"]}
```

Call it like a function anywhere in the server. It fetches once, then serves the cached result to every output that needs it.

::: {.callout-note}
Without `reactive.calc`, the same API call would fire separately for every output that uses the data.
:::

---

## Let's go! {.center}

Open Positron (or another IDE). We're starting from a blank file.

```
app/
├── basic.py      ← we build this together
├── polished.py   ← brand.yml, custom CSS, pitch background
└── _brand.yml    ← shared with these slides
```

You can check everything on GitHub: hypebright/kickoff-with-shiny-for-python
